learningOutcomes: Define demand and supply and explain the law of demand and supply
Identify factors that shift demand and supply curves
Explain how prices are determined in a market
realLifeContexts: Why petrol prices rise when crude oil supply falls
Why umbrellas get expensive during monsoon
How farmer income is affected by a bumper crop
commonMisconceptions: A rise in price causes demand to fall — demand curve shift vs. movement along curve
Supply and quantity supplied are the same
Equilibrium means both buyers and sellers are completely happy

learningOutcomes: Calculate and interpret price elasticity of demand and supply
Distinguish between elastic, inelastic, and unit elastic demand
Explain cross elasticity and income elasticity of demand
Analyze consumer and producer surplus
realLifeContexts: Why taxing cigarettes raises revenue but taxing luxury goods does not (inelastic vs. elastic)
How airlines price seats differently using elasticity
Why farmers benefit from inelastic food demand even with supply shocks
commonMisconceptions: Steeper demand curve always means more inelastic
Necessities are always perfectly inelastic
PED is always negative so it is meaningless

learningOutcomes: Derive demand from indifference curve analysis
Analyze market failure due to externalities and public goods
Evaluate government interventions: price floors, ceilings, taxes, and subsidies
Apply demand-supply analysis to factor markets
realLifeContexts: Minimum wage as a price floor and its employment effects
Carbon tax as a Pigouvian tax for pollution externality
Rent control in cities creating housing shortages
commonMisconceptions: Subsidies always help consumers without any cost to efficiency
A price ceiling below equilibrium always helps all buyers
Externalities are only negative — positive externalities are less important

learningOutcomes: Identify the four main market structures
Explain features of perfect competition and monopoly
Describe how firms make output and pricing decisions
realLifeContexts: Why electricity is a natural monopoly in most countries
How a local market with many similar shops resembles perfect competition
Why tech giants like Google and Meta behave like oligopolies
commonMisconceptions: Perfect competition is the most common market structure in reality
Monopolies always earn profit
All oligopolies collude with each other

learningOutcomes: Use marginal revenue and marginal cost to find profit-maximizing output
Compare long-run and short-run equilibrium across market structures
Explain price discrimination and conditions required
Analyze the kinked demand curve model in oligopoly
realLifeContexts: Why airlines charge different prices for the same seat (price discrimination)
OPEC as a cartel controlling oil prices
How dominant firms in tech use predatory pricing
commonMisconceptions: MR = price for all market structures
Price discrimination is always illegal
Long-run equilibrium in perfect competition means no firms make any money

learningOutcomes: Apply game theory to strategic firm behavior
Evaluate welfare effects of monopoly vs. perfect competition
Analyze contestable markets and natural monopoly regulation
Assess merger policy and antitrust economics
realLifeContexts: Why Facebook acquired WhatsApp and Instagram — antitrust concerns
UK water utility regulation using RPI-X
Bertrand paradox in telecom pricing wars
commonMisconceptions: Contestable markets require many firms to be competitive
Natural monopolies should always be nationalized
Game theory always predicts collusion in oligopolies

learningOutcomes: Define GDP and explain its significance
Distinguish between GDP, GNP, and NNP
Explain the circular flow of income
realLifeContexts: Why India's GDP growth rate matters for jobs and investment
Why per capita GDP is a better measure of living standards than total GDP
How COVID-19 reduced India's GDP
commonMisconceptions: GDP measures happiness and well-being directly
GDP and GNP are always the same
Higher GDP always means better quality of life

learningOutcomes: Calculate national income using expenditure, income, and output methods
Explain the multiplier effect
Distinguish between nominal and real GDP
Analyze injections and leakages in the circular flow
realLifeContexts: Why government spending during recession multiplies through the economy
How inflation distorts GDP comparisons over time
Why exports are injections and imports are leakages
commonMisconceptions: The multiplier effect is always greater than 1
Nominal GDP always rises, so real GDP must also rise
All three methods of calculating GDP give the same result in practice without adjustments

learningOutcomes: Analyze limitations of GDP as a welfare measure
Apply the Keynesian model to explain output gaps
Evaluate alternative welfare measures (HDI, Genuine Progress Indicator)
Assess the relationship between inequality and national income distribution
realLifeContexts: Why India ranks low on HDI despite high GDP growth
How income inequality is measured using Gini coefficient
Why illegal activity and household work are excluded from GDP
commonMisconceptions: HDI is always a better measure than GDP for all purposes
A Gini coefficient of 0 is always desirable
Increasing national income always reduces poverty

learningOutcomes: Define money and explain its functions
Describe the structure of the banking system
Explain how banks create credit
realLifeContexts: Why a barter system is inefficient compared to money
How a savings account in a bank helps the economy grow
Why RBI controls how much banks can lend
commonMisconceptions: Banks lend out exactly the deposits they receive
The central bank controls all money in circulation directly
Digital money is not real money

learningOutcomes: Explain the money multiplier and credit creation process
Describe tools of monetary policy (repo rate, CRR, SLR, OMO)
Distinguish between M1, M2, and M3 money supply
Analyze the relationship between money supply and inflation
realLifeContexts: How RBI raising repo rate reduces inflation in India
Why printing money excessively causes hyperinflation (Zimbabwe example)
How demonetization affected the money supply
commonMisconceptions: Raising repo rate directly reduces money supply immediately
Central banks can perfectly control inflation using interest rates
CRR and SLR are the same instrument

learningOutcomes: Apply the IS-LM model to analyze monetary and fiscal policy interactions
Evaluate inflation targeting as a monetary policy framework
Analyze unconventional monetary policies (QE, negative interest rates)
Assess the role of financial markets in transmitting monetary policy
realLifeContexts: How US Federal Reserve QE after 2008 crisis stabilized the economy
Why Japan struggled with deflation despite zero interest rates (liquidity trap)
India's shift to inflation targeting framework under RBI
commonMisconceptions: The IS-LM model is adequate for all macroeconomic analysis
QE always causes hyperinflation
Negative interest rates incentivize all banks to lend more

learningOutcomes: Define fiscal policy and distinguish between taxation and government spending
Explain the government budget: surplus, deficit, and balance
Describe how fiscal policy can be used to stabilize the economy
realLifeContexts: How government spending on infrastructure creates jobs
Why India's Union Budget matters for economic growth
How tax cuts affect consumption and investment
commonMisconceptions: Government must always have a balanced budget
Tax cuts always increase government revenue (Laffer curve oversimplification)
Fiscal deficit is always harmful to the economy

learningOutcomes: Explain the fiscal multiplier and its determinants
Distinguish between discretionary and automatic stabilizers
Analyze the crowding out effect of government borrowing
Evaluate the impact of fiscal policy on AD-AS model
realLifeContexts: How unemployment benefits act as automatic stabilizers
Why India's MGNREGA acts as a fiscal stabilizer
Crowding out in India's capital market from high government borrowing
commonMisconceptions: Automatic stabilizers require government decision each time they activate
Crowding out always completely offsets fiscal stimulus
A larger fiscal multiplier always means better fiscal policy effectiveness

learningOutcomes: Apply debt sustainability analysis to government borrowing
Evaluate the Laffer curve and optimal taxation theory
Assess fiscal consolidation strategies and their trade-offs
Analyze inter-generational equity and long-run fiscal implications
realLifeContexts: Why Greece's debt crisis offers lessons for fiscal sustainability
India's FRBM targets and fiscal consolidation challenges
How pension obligations create long-run fiscal risks
commonMisconceptions: The Laffer curve always has a peak at 50% tax rate
Debt-to-GDP ratio is the only measure of fiscal sustainability
Fiscal consolidation always reduces growth in the short run

learningOutcomes: Explain why countries trade with each other
Define absolute advantage and comparative advantage
Describe the basic components of the balance of payments
realLifeContexts: Why India exports software services while importing crude oil
Why even a highly productive country benefits from trade
How trade deficit affects India's currency
commonMisconceptions: A trade deficit is always harmful to an economy
Countries with absolute advantage in everything should produce everything themselves
Free trade benefits all citizens equally

learningOutcomes: Analyze the terms of trade and their effects on welfare
Evaluate trade policy instruments: tariffs, quotas, and subsidies
Explain exchange rate determination and purchasing power parity
Describe the WTO and regional trade agreements
realLifeContexts: How US tariffs on Chinese goods affected global supply chains
Why the rupee's depreciation makes exports cheaper and imports costlier
India's FTA with ASEAN and its trade effects
commonMisconceptions: Tariffs always protect domestic jobs without any economic cost
PPP exchange rate equals the market exchange rate
Trade liberalization always increases inequality

learningOutcomes: Apply Heckscher-Ohlin theory and evaluate its limitations
Analyze the J-curve effect on trade balance after devaluation
Evaluate strategic trade policy arguments
Assess global value chains and their trade implications
realLifeContexts: Why Bangladesh exports garments (labor-abundant country — H-O theorem)
J-curve effect seen after India's rupee depreciation episodes
Apple's global value chain involving 40+ countries
commonMisconceptions: Heckscher-Ohlin perfectly predicts trade patterns in all cases (Leontief paradox)
Devaluation immediately improves the trade balance
Strategic trade policy has no risks of retaliation

learningOutcomes: Explain the concept of utility and marginal utility
State the law of diminishing marginal utility
Describe how a consumer achieves equilibrium
realLifeContexts: Why eating the 5th slice of pizza gives less satisfaction than the 1st
How a student decides to allocate pocket money between different goods
Why water is essential but cheap while diamonds are not essential but expensive (diamond-water paradox)
commonMisconceptions: Marginal utility can never be negative
Total utility keeps rising as long as marginal utility is positive
Consumer equilibrium means the consumer is spending all income on one good

learningOutcomes: Use indifference curves and budget lines to find consumer equilibrium
Explain income and substitution effects
Distinguish normal, inferior, and Giffen goods
Derive a demand curve from consumer equilibrium
realLifeContexts: Why a price fall in a normal good increases quantity demanded via both effects
How rice consumption in poor rural areas behaves as a Giffen good
Income effect of a wage rise on leisure vs. work choice
commonMisconceptions: Indifference curves can intersect without any logical problem
All inferior goods are Giffen goods
The substitution effect alone explains the downward slope of the demand curve

learningOutcomes: Apply revealed preference theory
Analyze consumer behavior under uncertainty (expected utility theory)
Evaluate behavioral economics departures from rational choice
Apply the Slutsky equation to decompose price effects
realLifeContexts: How insurance markets reflect risk aversion
Why people prefer guaranteed ₹100 over 50% chance of ₹250 (expected utility)
How supermarket layout nudges consumer choices (behavioral economics)
commonMisconceptions: Rational consumers always maximize utility with perfect information
Risk-neutral behavior is the most common in practice
Behavioral economics invalidates all of standard consumer theory

learningOutcomes: Explain the factors of production
Define fixed and variable costs
Describe the short run and long run in production
realLifeContexts: Why a restaurant has fixed rent regardless of customers
How a factory decides to hire more workers vs. buy more machines
Why a new business may make losses initially
commonMisconceptions: In the short run, all costs are fixed
The long run is always exactly one year
Profit equals total revenue minus only variable costs

learningOutcomes: Apply the law of diminishing returns
Calculate and graph average and marginal cost curves
Explain economies and diseconomies of scale
Relate isoquants and isocosts to producer equilibrium
realLifeContexts: Why call centers have U-shaped ATC curves at different output levels
How Amazon achieves economies of scale in logistics
Why small farms often have higher average costs than large agribusinesses
commonMisconceptions: MC curve intersects ATC at the minimum of MC
Economies of scale continue indefinitely
Long-run AC curve is always below short-run AC at every output level

learningOutcomes: Derive cost functions from production functions mathematically
Apply the envelope theorem to long-run cost curves
Analyze multi-product cost functions and economies of scope
Evaluate X-inefficiency and principal-agent problems in production
realLifeContexts: Why banks offer both insurance and loans (economies of scope)
How X-inefficiency explains poor performance of state-owned enterprises
Total factor productivity as a measure of innovation and efficiency growth
commonMisconceptions: Economies of scope and economies of scale are the same phenomenon
X-inefficiency only occurs in monopolies
Returns to scale and returns to a factor are the same concept
